   1 | # CodeTether Agent

   6 | 
   7 | A high-performance AI coding agent written in Rust. First-class A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol support with dual JSON-RPC + gRPC transports, in-process agent message bus, rich terminal UI, parallel swarm execution, autonomous PRD-driven development, and a local FunctionGemma tool-call router that separates reasoning from formatting.
   8 | 
   9 | ## v4.0.0 — Major Release: Hybrid Swarm, Local CUDA & Real-Time Task Dispatch
  10 | 
  11 | CodeTether Agent `v4.0.0` is a major release delivering zero-latency local inference, Kubernetes-native real-time task dispatch, and a significantly reduced cloud cost model.
  12 | 
  13 | - **Hybrid Swarm Architecture & Local CUDA Provider** — New `LocalCudaProvider` runs ML inference directly on NVIDIA hardware via Candle, achieving zero network latency. Strategic reasoning stays on cloud models (e.g., Claude Opus); tool-call formatting is offloaded to local FunctionGemma. Full architecture documented in `docs/architecture/hybrid_swarm.md`.
  14 | - **CloudEvent Task Notification** — Worker now receives task notifications via Knative Eventing. The `/task` endpoint extracts `task_id` from the CloudEvent payload and immediately polls for pending tasks, eliminating SSE polling delay.
  15 | - **Claude Opus 4.6 Bedrock Pricing** — Reflects new Amazon Bedrock rates: input $5.00/1M tokens (was $15.00, −67%) and output $25.00/1M tokens (was $75.00, −67%). 200 K context limit added for Opus 4.6. TUI token display updated.
  16 | - **Windows Installer Fix** — `install.ps1` now tries multiple artifact formats (msvc + zip for GitHub Actions, gnu + tar.gz for Jenkins) and auto-detects the correct binary. Improved error messages when no binary is available.
  17 | ### v4.0.1 — Swarm Reliability & Morph Backend
  18 | 
  19 | - **Morph AI Backend** — Integrated Morph AI backend into `edit` and `multiedit` tools for intelligent, AI-assisted code modifications with fallback to exact string replacement.
  20 | - **Swarm Retry Improvements** — Refactored retry logic with configurable `base_delay_ms` and `max_delay_ms` parameters. Added `retry_count` to `SubTaskResult` for tracking retry attempts.
  21 | - **Forage Enhancements** — Added `--no-s3` flag for local-only execution. Improved OKR seeding with moonshot-derived objectives. KR progress now requires quality gates to pass.
  22 | - **Memory Optimizations** — `SessionEvent::SessionSync` and `AutochatUiEvent::AgentEvent` now use heap allocation via Box.
  23 | 
  24 | ### Upgrading to v4.0
  25 | 
  26 | - Existing configurations, Vault secrets, and sessions are forward-compatible with v4.
  27 | - Worker deployments automatically gain CloudEvent push-based dispatch; no `--server` polling changes required.
  28 | - Enable local CUDA inference by building with `--features candle-cuda` and setting `CODETETHER_TOOL_ROUTER_DEVICE=cuda`.
  29 | - Amazon Bedrock users: update cost-tracking dashboards to reflect the new per-token rates.
  30 | - Optional Bedrock service tier overrides: set `CODETETHER_BEDROCK_SERVICE_TIER` (provider path) or `CODETETHER_COGNITION_THINKER_BEDROCK_SERVICE_TIER` (cognition thinker path) to values like `standard`, `priority`, or `flex`.
  31 | 
  32 | ## Notable Prior Milestones
  33 | 
  34 | ### v2.0.3 — Strategic Execution + Durable Replay
  35 | 
  36 | - **OKR-Gated `/go` Workflow** — Strategic execution with draft → approve/deny → run semantics and persisted OKR run tracking.
  37 | - **OKR CLI & Reporting** — `codetether okr` command group: `list`, `status`, `create`, `runs`, `export`, `stats`, `report`.
  38 | - **Relay Checkpointing + Resume** — In-flight relay state checkpointed for crash recovery and exact-order continuation.
  39 | - **Event Stream Module** — Structured JSONL event sourcing with byte-range offsets for efficient replay.
  40 | - **S3/R2 Archival** — Optional archival pipeline for event streams and chat artifacts to S3-compatible storage.
  41 | - **Correlation-Rich Observability** — Audit/event models include `okr_id`, `okr_run_id`, `relay_id`, `session_id`.
  42 | - **Worker HTTP Probe Server** — `/health`, `/ready`, `/worker/status` endpoints for Kubernetes integration.
  43 | 
  44 | ### v1.1.6-alpha-2 — Agent Bus & gRPC Transport
  45 | 
  46 | This release adds the inter-agent communication backbone — a broadcast-based in-process message bus and a gRPC transport layer implementing the full A2A protocol, enabling high-frequency agent-to-agent communication both locally and across the network.
  47 | 
  48 | - **Agent Message Bus** — Central `AgentBus` with topic-based pub/sub routing (`agent.{id}`, `task.{id}`, `swarm.{id}`, `broadcast`). Every local agent gets a zero-copy `BusHandle` for send/receive. Supports task updates, artifact sharing, tool dispatch, heartbeats, and free-form inter-agent messaging.
  49 | - **Agent Registry** — `AgentRegistry` tracks connected agents via `AgentCard`. Ephemeral card factory for short-lived sub-agents with `bus://local/{name}` URLs.
  50 | - **gRPC Transport (A2A Protocol)** — Full tonic-based gRPC server implementing all 9 A2A RPCs: `SendMessage`, `SendStreamingMessage`, `GetTask`, `CancelTask`, `TaskSubscription`, push notification config CRUD, and `GetAgentCard`. Shares state with JSON-RPC via `GrpcTaskStore`.

1 | # Product Requirements Document (PRD)
   2 | 
   3 | ## CodeTether Agent
   4 | 
   5 | **Version:** 1.0  
   6 | **Date:** February 2025  
   7 | **Status:** In Development  
   8 | **Author:** CodeTether Team  
   9 | 
  10 | ---
  11 | 
  12 | ## 1. Executive Summary
  13 | 
  14 | ### 1.1 Product Vision Statement
  15 | 
  16 | > **CodeTether Agent is a high-performance, A2A-native AI coding agent that autonomously implements software features through PRD-driven development, enabling developers to transform product specifications into production-ready code with minimal human intervention.**
  17 | 
  18 | CodeTether Agent represents a paradigm shift in AI-assisted software development. Built from the ground up in Rust for maximum performance and reliability, it serves as both an intelligent coding companion and an autonomous implementation engine. The agent combines the power of multiple AI providers with a sophisticated tool system, Language Server Protocol (LSP) integration, and recursive language model processing to handle complex development tasks at unprecedented speed and scale.
  19 | 
  20 | Unlike traditional AI coding assistants that require constant human guidance, CodeTether Agent can operate autonomously through its "Ralph" loop—reading product requirements, implementing features, running quality checks, and iterating until all acceptance criteria are met. This capability has been proven through dogfooding: the agent implemented 20 of its own user stories (LSP client, RLM pool, truncation utilities, and more) with a 100% pass rate on quality checks.
  21 | 
  22 | ### 1.2 Key Objectives for the Three Main Features
  23 | 
  24 | #### Feature 1: Autonomous PRD-Driven Development (Ralph)
  25 | 
  26 | **Objective:** Enable fully autonomous feature implementation from structured product requirements documents, eliminating the manual overhead of translating specifications into code.
  27 | 
  28 | **Key Goals:**
  29 | - Parse structured PRD documents containing user stories with acceptance criteria, priorities, and dependencies
  30 | - Automatically select and implement the highest-priority stories with satisfied dependencies
  31 | - Execute comprehensive quality checks (type checking, linting, testing, building) after each implementation
  32 | - Maintain memory across iterations through git history, progress tracking, and PRD state management
  33 | - Achieve 100% pass rate on all quality checks for implemented stories
  34 | - Reduce feature implementation time by 163x compared to manual development
  35 | 
  36 | #### Feature 2: Language Server Protocol (LSP) Client Integration
  37 | 
  38 | **Objective:** Provide first-class code intelligence capabilities by implementing a complete LSP client that communicates with language servers for real-time code analysis.
  39 | 
  40 | **Key Goals:**
  41 | - Implement full LSP transport layer using stdio with proper JSON-RPC message handling
  42 | - Support core LSP lifecycle: initialize handshake, document synchronization, and graceful shutdown
  43 | - Enable go-to-definition, find-references, hover information, and code completion requests
  44 | - Manage multiple concurrent LSP connections for polyglot development environments
  45 | - Maintain server health monitoring with automatic restart capabilities
  46 | - Support industry-standard language servers (rust-analyzer, typescript-language-server, etc.)
  47 | 
  48 | #### Feature 3: Recursive Language Model (RLM) Processing
  49 | 
  50 | **Objective:** Handle contexts that exceed model window limits through intelligent chunking and recursive analysis, enabling the agent to work with arbitrarily large codebases and documents.
  51 | 
  52 | **Key Goals:**
  53 | - Implement intelligent content chunking based on content type (code, logs, documents, conversations)
  54 | - Create an internal REPL-like environment where the LLM can explore content programmatically
  55 | - Enable sub-LM queries for semantic questions across chunks
  56 | - Synthesize coherent answers from multiple chunk analyses
  57 | - Provide connection pooling for RLM agents to minimize latency and resource usage
  58 | - Support parallel processing of independent chunks for improved throughput
  59 | 
  60 | ### 1.3 Success Metrics
  61 | 
  62 | | Metric Category | Metric | Target | Measurement Method |
  63 | |-----------------|--------|--------|-------------------|
  64 | | **Autonomous Development** | Story implementation pass rate | ≥95% | Quality check results from `cargo check`, `cargo clippy`, `cargo test`, `cargo build` |
  65 | | | Average time per user story | <5 minutes | Timestamp analysis from PRD iteration logs |
  66 | | | Token efficiency vs. manual coding | 3x reduction | Token usage comparison with baseline |
  67 | | **LSP Integration** | Language server compatibility | 5+ servers | Verified with rust-analyzer, typescript-language-server, pylsp, gopls, clangd |
  68 | | | Request-response latency | <100ms p95 | Instrumented timing on LSP operations |
  69 | | | Server uptime (with auto-restart) | 99.9% | Health check monitoring over 7-day period |
  70 | | **RLM Processing** | Maximum context size handled | 10MB+ | Stress testing with large files |
  71 | | | Chunk processing throughput | >100 chunks/sec | Benchmark with standard test corpus |
  72 | | | Answer accuracy on large contexts | ≥90% | Human evaluation on 50 test queries |
  73 | | **Performance** | Binary startup time | <15ms | `time codetether --help` |
  74 | | | Memory usage (idle) | <20MB | `ps` memory reporting |
  75 | | | Memory usage (swarm, 10 agents) | <60MB | Peak RSS during swarm execution |
  76 | | | Sub-agent spawn time | <2ms | Instrumented spawn latency |
  77 | | **Adoption** | Quality gate pass rate | 100% | All stories must pass 4 quality checks |
  78 | | | Documentation coverage | ≥80% | `cargo doc` coverage analysis |
  79 | | | Test coverage | ≥70% | `cargo tarpaulin` report |
  80 | 
  81 | ### 1.4 Target User Personas
  82 | 
  83 | #### Persona 1: Senior Software Engineer - "Alex"
  84 | 
  85 | **Background:** Alex is a senior engineer at a mid-sized tech company, working on a complex Rust codebase. They are highly productive but constantly face tight deadlines and technical debt.
  86 | 
  87 | **Goals:**
  88 | - Automate repetitive implementation tasks to focus on architecture and design
  89 | - Maintain high code quality standards without sacrificing velocity
  90 | - Quickly understand large, unfamiliar codebases
  91 | - Reduce time spent on boilerplate and test writing
  92 | 
  93 | **Pain Points:**
  94 | - Context switching between high-level design and low-level implementation
  95 | - Reviewing and refactoring code written by junior developers
  96 | - Navigating legacy code without proper documentation

1 | # CodeTether Hybrid Swarm: Oracle-First Architecture
   2 | 
   3 | This document is the current architecture source of truth for `codetether rlm` and oracle validation.
   4 | 
   5 | ## Objective
   6 | 
   7 | Run model-driven code navigation with deterministic verification always on, while persisting full validation traces for reproducible training data.
   8 | 
   9 | ## Locked Runtime Decisions
  10 | 
  11 | 1. `codetether rlm` is Oracle-First.
  12 | 2. Oracle classification runs by default on every RLM invocation.
  13 | 3. Oracle verdicts are non-blocking for process exit (`failed` verdict does not force non-zero exit).
  14 | 4. Static no-provider fallback is removed from `rlm`; provider resolution is fail-fast.
  15 | 5. Provider strategy is local-first (`local_cuda`), then OpenRouter fallback when no explicit provider is pinned.
  16 | 6. Default cloud model baseline is Qwen 3.5 family (`Qwen/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B`) unless overridden.
  17 | 7. Trace persistence writes full envelope records for all verdicts (`golden`, `failed`, `unverified`, `consensus`).
  18 | 8. Remote persistence target defaults to MinIO/S3 using Bus S3 credential resolution.
  19 | 9. Remote outages do not drop traces; traces spool locally and can be synced later.
  20 | 
  21 | ## `rlm` Command Flow
  22 | 
  23 | 1. Build source content from `--file` or `--content`.
  24 | 2. Resolve provider/model:
  25 |    - Explicit `--model provider/model`: use requested provider or fail.
  26 |    - Explicit `local_cuda/...` without runtime config fails (no silent fallback).
  27 |    - Unqualified model: try `local_cuda`, else `openrouter`.
  28 |    - No model: try `local_cuda` with local default, else `openrouter` with default Qwen model.
  29 | 3. Execute RLM analysis.
  30 | 4. Validate result through oracle (unless explicitly disabled with `--no-oracle-verify`).
  31 | 5. Persist full oracle envelope via spool-first storage pipeline.
  32 | 6. Print answer and oracle status.
  33 | 
  34 | ## Oracle CLI
  35 | 
  36 | ### `codetether oracle validate`
  37 | 
  38 | - Deterministically validates a provided FINAL payload against source.
  39 | - Supports `--file` or `--content`, and `--payload` or `--payload-file`.
  40 | - JSON mode emits verdict + full record envelope.
  41 | - Persistence is opt-in with `--persist`.
  42 | 
  43 | ### `codetether oracle sync`
  44 | 
  45 | - Flushes local pending spool records to configured MinIO/S3 target.
  46 | - Reports `uploaded`, `retained`, `failed`, and `pending_after`.
  47 | 
  48 | ## Persistence Semantics
  49 | 
  50 | ### Remote config resolution
  51 | 
  52 | Uses Bus S3 resolution order:
  53 | 
  54 | 1. `MINIO_*` / `CODETETHER_BUS_S3_*`
  55 | 2. `CODETETHER_CHAT_SYNC_MINIO_*`
  56 | 3. Vault `chat-sync-minio`
  57 | 
  58 | ### Local spool
  59 | 
  60 | - Default: `~/.codetether/traces/pending/`
  61 | - Override: `CODETETHER_ORACLE_SPOOL_DIR`
  62 | - Writes are atomic (temp file then rename).
  63 | 
  64 | ### Upload behavior
  65 | 
  66 | 1. Write local spool file first.
  67 | 2. Attempt remote upload.
  68 | 3. On success, remove local spool file.
  69 | 4. On failure, keep local spool file and report warning.
  70 | 
  71 | ## Output Contracts
  72 | 
  73 | ### Non-JSON `codetether rlm`
  74 | 
  75 | - Prints normal answer.
  76 | - Always appends one oracle status line:
  77 |   - `[oracle: golden ✓] ...`
  78 |   - `[oracle: failed ✗] ...`
  79 |   - `[oracle: unverified —] ...`
  80 |   - `[oracle: consensus ✓] ...`
  81 | 
  82 | ### JSON `codetether rlm`
  83 | 
  84 | - Always includes `oracle` object with verdict metadata and persistence state.
  85 | 
  86 | ## Compatibility Notes
  87 | 
  88 | - `--oracle-verify` remains accepted for compatibility but is deprecated and no-op.
  89 | - Use `--no-oracle-verify` for explicit emergency opt-out.
  90 |
